How cheap is dirt [Music] cheap? These five wines are so cheap yet so high quality that it's genuinely unclear how any of these wine producers are able to turn a profit. Before I reveal the price though, let's talk about the relationship between price and quality in wine-making. The truth is there's an incredibly strong correlation between price and quality, but only up to about 50 bucks or so. In other words, there's a nonlinear relationship between price and quality when purchasing wines. The reason for this is that in order to make great wine, there are a few things you have to do that are non-negotiable, and those things are pretty expensive. First, you need to cultivate and harvest highquality grapes by hand. Sure, you can use machines for this, and on average, a single mechanical harvester can do the work of about 50 to 70 people over the same time period, but this comes at the expense of quality. As industrialized farm equipment is not as effective as experienced vineyard management workers and identifying and removing underripe, overripe, and rotten grapes, this means bad rotten grapes are making their way into the wine- making process. Imagine if I added just an ounce of moldy, completely rotten milk to your otherwise brand new carton. Would you want to drink it? It's hard to understate how significant the aging process is to the quality of wine. Both the type of vessel the wine is aged in, like an oak barrel versus a giant stainless steel vat, and the amount of time it stays in the vessel to age will effectively create the taste of the wine. Oak barrels can cost up to $1,500 a piece and only hold about 300 standard bottles of wine. That means just the barrel alone accounts for up to $5 of the cost of the wine. Meanwhile, cheap wines typically use industrial stainless steel vats that can be reused over and over and over again. and don't impart any flavor or character into the wine during the aging process. They need those wine sales to really make a living.  Especially if it is a small family run place. The food is often kept cheaper so that wine sales bring in more profits.

- This Country Invented Wine 8,000 Years Ago | Georgia (Not the State): Kind: captions Language: en Nestled between the towering [music] peaks of the greater and lesser Caucasus mountains lies a land of breathtaking beauty and ancient secrets. [music] Its geography is a dramatic tapestry of snowcapped summits, lush river valleys, and sundrenched [music] coastlines along the Black Sea. For millennia, this strategic position [music] has made it a bridge, a prize, and a sanctuary. [music] The air is crisp, carrying the scent of wild herbs and damp earth. Below, [music] a patchwork of vineyards stretches as far as the eye can see, cradled by the formidable wall of the caucuses. This is not [music] just a landscape. It is a living history book. It is a place where nature and humanity have been entwined for thousands of years in a delicate and enduring dance. [music] The very soil seems to hum with the echoes of ancient feasts, forgotten kingdoms, and [music] the quiet, persistent work of generations who have called this remarkable land their home. This is [music] the stage upon which one of humanity's most cherished stories began. It is a story not of kings and [music] conquests, but of something far more intimate and universal. It is the story [music] of the grape. Long before the great civilizations of Greece and Rome toasted [music] their gods with wine, the people of this land were cultivating the wild vine, they were learning its secrets, transforming its fruit into a liquid that would come to symbolize celebration, spirituality, and life itself. Here in the heart of the Caucusus, humanity first fell in love with wine, a love affair that has now lasted for 8,000 years. In the quiet archaeological sites of Georgia's CMO Cartly region, something extraordinary was uncovered. Scientists carefully brushed away the soil of millennia from fragments of ancient pottery. These were not just any clay shards. Chemical analysis revealed the telltale traces of tartaric acid, the fingerprint of grapes and wine, dated to around 6,000 BC. These discoveries pushed back the known origins of wine- making by centuries. It was a profound revelation. Here in these Neolithic villages, our ancestors [music] were cultivating grapes and fermenting their juice, beginning a tradition that would circle the globe. It was the dawn of viticulture. What we found is not just the oldest evidence of wine. It is the evidence of a culture built [music] around wine. The sheer number of grape pips, the specialized pottery, it all points to [music] a people who had domesticated the wild Eurasian vine. Vitis vinifera sativa. They were not just gathering wild fruit. They were farmers, innovators. They were laying the foundation for a relationship [music] with the vine that would define their identity for the next 8 millennia. It is a connection that is almost genetic. >> This was not a fleeting experiment. It was the beginning of a deep unbroken tradition. These early people developed a unique method for making and storing their wine. A technique so effective that [music] it is still used today. They understood that to make good wine, you must work with the earth, not against it. [music] They look to the soil not just to grow their grapes but also to nurture their wine to protect it and allow it to transform. This ancient wisdom passed down from one generation to the next became the cornerstone [music] of Georgian life and culture. A sacred trust held for thousands of years. The evidence is overwhelming. Georgia is without question the cradle of wine. The word [music] for wine itself, Gavino, is believed to be the root from which the word has spread into countless other languages. This is not merely a point of national [music] pride for Georgians. It is a fundamental part of who they are. To understand Georgia, one must understand [music] its wine. It flows not just in their glasses, but through their history, their poetry, their songs, and their souls. It is the lifeblood of the nation, a gift from their ancestors and a legacy they continue to share with the world. At the heart of Georgia's 8,000-year-old wine- makingaking tradition [music] is a remarkable vessel, the KVI. These are not simply barrels or tanks. They are enormous egg-shaped clay amphai handmade by master craftsmen using ancient techniques. A kvery is a living thing, a womb for the wine. Once shaped and fired in a kiln, it is coated on the inside with beeswax and then buried [music] completely in the earth with only its neck protruding from the floor of the [music] winery, known as a marin. This subterranean cellar provides a constant cool temperature, allowing the wine to ferment and age in perfect stability. The process is one of natural magic. Grapes often along with their skins, stems and pips are crushed and poured into the cevry. [music] The natural yeasts present on the grape skins kickstart the fermentation. The vessel's conical shape plays a crucial role. As fermentation proceeds, the solid matter, the skins and pips known as chacha is [music] constantly circulated by the rising carbon dioxide. This allows for the gentle extraction of color, tannins, and flavor [music] compounds, giving Georgian cavevery wines their distinctive character, structure, [music] and amber or deep red hues. It is a method of beautiful simplicity and profound complexity. You must feel the clay. It has a memory. Each cave has its own voice, its own soul. It takes weeks to build one. Layer by layer, coil by coil. There are no machines, only hands and knowledge passed from my grandfather to my father and from my father to me. We are not just making a pot. We are creating a home for the wine. It must be strong enough to hold the pressure yet porous enough to breathe. >> Once fermentation is complete, the solid matter slowly settles to the pointed bottom of the crevery, forming a natural filter. The wine above clarifies naturally, aging on its [music] leaves for months, sometimes years. This extended skin contact and slow stable maturation in the earth is what makes wine so unique. It is a pure expression of the grape and the [music] land. In 2013, UNESCO recognized the ancient Georgian traditional wine-making method as an intangible cultural heritage of humanity, a testament to its enduring importance and the wisdom of its creators. Georgia's story is one of incredible survival, and nowhere is this more evident than in its vineyards. The country is home to over 500 indigenous grape varieties, a staggering diversity found nowhere else on Earth. This botanical treasure trove is a direct result of Georgia's geography and history. The Caucusus mountains acted as a natural barrier, isolating the region and protecting its ancient vines from the diseases such as filera that devastated European vineyards in the 19th century. This isolation created a kind of viticultural arc, preserving a genetic library of grapes that stretches back to the dawn of wine- making. This survival was not just a matter of luck. It was a conscious act of cultural preservation. Throughout centuries of invasion and occupation, [music] Georgian monks painstakingly cultivated and cataloged these grape varieties in monastery vineyards. For them, wine was not merely a beverage. It was a sacred element of the Orthodox Christian liturgy and a symbol of national identity. In times of oppression, tending to the saparavi, rkatsitelli or mitzvane vines was an act of defiance, a quiet insistence that Georgian culture would endure. Each grape variety tells a story of a specific region, a specific flavor, a specific [music] piece of the Georgian soul. The Soviet era presented one of the greatest threats. Moscow's central planners demanded mass production, not diversity. They ordered vineyards to be ripped up and replanted with a mere handful of high yield approved grape varieties. Hundreds of ancient unique vines were pushed to the brink of extinction. Yet, even during [music] this dark period, defiant farmers and scientists kept the old varieties alive in small hidden plots and research institutes. [music] They understood they were the custodians of a priceless heritage and they risked their livelihoods to protect it for future generations. It was a silent botanical rebellion. >> To lose a grape variety is like losing a word from our language. It diminishes our ability to tell our story. My mission is to find these forgotten vines, the [music] Jani, the Simonosi, the Messuri Mitzvan, and give them a voice again. Each one offers a different taste of our land, [music] a different connection to our past. We are not just making wine. We are rebuilding our library of flavors. To walk through Georgia is honestly to walk through layers of history [music] recorded not in books, but in stone. The country's architecture is a dramatic chronicle of its triumphs, its faith, and its unyielding will to survive. High on a rocky bank overlooking the Matgari River stands [music] uplist, the Lord's Fortress. This is one of the oldest urban settlements in Georgia, [music] a sprawling cave city carved directly into the sandstone cliffs dating back to the early Iron Age. Its winding tunnels, rockh chambers, and pagan temples tell of a time before Christianity, a time of fire worship and ancient rituals. It is a powerful ghostly reminder of Georgia's deep past. Then came the cross. The arrival [music] of Christianity in the 4th century transformed Georgia forever and its architecture became a testament to [music] this profound new faith. In the heart of the ancient capital, Monskeeta [music] standset Coali Cathedral, the cathedral of the living pillar. According to [music] legend, it is built over the burial site of Christ's mantle. Rebuilt and expanded over centuries, its magnificent stone walls [music] have witnessed coronations, royal weddings, and the turmoil of invasions. It is the spiritual [music] heart of the nation, a symbol of the fusion of royal power and divine faith that has defined Georgia for over 1,500 [music] years. Perhaps the most astonishing of these stone chronicles is Vardzia. [music] This is not simply a monastery. It is a vertical city, a fortress against invasion, carved into the side of a mountain in the 13th century during Georgia's [music] golden age under Queen Tamar. It once contained over 3,000 apartments on 13 [music] levels, complete with a throne room, a pharmacy, and a complex [music] irrigation system. When Mongol invaders swept across the [music] land, the monks of Vadzia could retreat into their mountain stronghold, pulling up the hidden entrances [music] behind them. It is an all inspiring feat of engineering and a powerful symbol of Georgian resilience and ingenuity in the face of overwhelming odds. These sites are more than just [music] historical monuments. They are living parts of the national consciousness. They are places of [music] pilgrimage, of national pride, and of quiet contemplation. >> When Georgians look at these walls, they don't just see old stones. They see the faces of their ancestors. They feel the presence of Queen Tamar, the faith of Saint Nino, who brought Christianity, the defiance of the monks at Vardzia. This architecture connects them directly to their past. It reminds them that they come from a long line of survivors, of builders, of believers. These stones are their story. >> In Georgia, a feast or supra [music] is far more than just a meal. It's, you know, a profound cultural ritual, a rich and complex performance that binds [music] together family, friends, and community. The table groans under the weight [music] of countless dishes. Glistening katcha puri. That cheese-filled bread pulling apart in those satisfying strings. Succulent kinkali. those spiced meat dumplings you eat by hand, vibrant salads [music] of walnuts and pomegranates, and of course, smoky grilled meats. But the food, as magnificent as it is, is really only the backdrop. [music] The true purpose of the Supra is communication, the strengthening of bonds and the celebration of life itself, all orchestrated through [music] wine and words. The atmosphere is just electric, filled with conversation, [music] laughter, and song. It is a democratic and inclusive event. Everyone from the oldest patriarch to the youngest child has a place and a voice. The Supra is where news [music] is shared, problems are solved, friendships are forged, and memories are made. It's honestly [music] a powerful antidote to the isolation of modern life, a reminder of the deep human need [music] for connection. In a world that often feels fragmented, the supra stands as an act of deliberate community [music] building, a way of reaffirming that we are all in this together. It is a feast for the soul as much as for the body. Wine, of course, [music] is the essential lubricant of the supra. It's not just sipped idly, but is consumed in a structured way, always following [music] a toast. The wine, often a homemade vintage from the family's own cave, [music] is really seen as a sacred medium. It's the blood of the earth, a liquid that opens the heart and allows for honest and [music] meaningful expression. As the glasses are raised again and again, barriers [music] just fall away. People speak from the heart, sharing their hopes, [music] their sorrows, and their gratitude. The wine helps to elevate the gathering from a simple meal to something deeply spiritual and well transformative. The Supra is a microcosm [music] of Georgian culture. It embodies the nation's legendary hospitality, its love of life, its deep respect for tradition, and its emotional openness. A guest at a supra [music] is considered a gift from God and they are treated with incredible warmth and generosity. To be invited [music] to a family supra is to be given a glimpse into the very heart of Georgia. [music] It's an experience that stays with you long after the last plate has been cleared and the last song has been sung. A powerful testament [music] to the enduring importance of food, wine, and fellowship in knitting together the fabric of human society. Presiding over every supra is a vital figure, the tomada or toastmaster. This is not just the person who proposes the toasts. They are the philosophical and emotional leader of the feast. The role is an honor and a great responsibility requiring wit, eloquence, and a deep understanding of human nature. The tamada is chosen by the host or the guests and once appointed their authority is absolute. They guide the evening's emotional journey using a series of toasts [music] to weave together themes of family, homeland, peace, love, [music] and remembrance. A good tomato can make a simple dinner feel like a profound spiritual event. The toasts are structured and follow a traditional order. The first is always [music] to God, giving thanks for life and for the gathering. This is followed by a toast to Georgia, the homeland. Then the tomato will toast to peace. A deeply felt wish in a country that has known so much conflict. From there they will move to more personal subjects. Toasts to the parents, to the children, to the memory of those who have passed away, to the women who prepared the feast, to guests, and to the reason [music] for the gathering. Each toast is a short improvised speech. [music] A piece of spontaneous poetry that connects the universal to the personal. The tomata is a bridge. A bridge between the past and the present. Between the people at the table and between the table and the divine. My job is not just to say cheers. My job is to make people think, to make them feel. With each toast, I try to open a new door in their hearts. A toast to our ancestors reminds us where we come from. A toast to our children reminds us why we live. It is a great art. You must be a poet, a philosopher, and a psychologist all at once. After the tomada delivers a toast, everyone at the table [music] raises their glass and drinks. It's a collective act of affirmation, an agreement with the sentiment expressed. The toasts are not rushed. There are long pauses between them for food, conversation, and [music] quite often polyonic singing. The songs like the toasts are filled with emotion and history. This intricate dance of words, wine, and music is what gives the supra its unique power. It is a living tradition that elevates a meal into a moving and unforgettable celebration of what it means to be human, connected, and alive. In the bustling streets of Telisi and the quiet villages of Cati, a fascinating synthesis is taking place. Modern Georgia is a vibrant landscape where ancient traditions and cuttingedge innovations coexist and enrich one another. The younger generation, deeply proud of their heritage, is finding new and exciting ways to express their Georgian identity. They are not abandoning the past. They are reimagining it for the 21st century. This blend of old and new is creating a dynamic cultural scene that is attracting attention from around the world. It's a new vintage built on an ancient roottock. This is, you know, wonderfully visible in the world of wine. While many wine makers are passionately reviving the 8,000year-old kuvery method, they are doing so with a modern understanding of science and hygiene. They are combining ancestral wisdom with contemporary knowledge to create wines of incredible purity and complexity. Alongside these traditionalists, a new wave of ventners is experimenting with modern techniques creating sleek European style wines that showcase the unique character of Georgian grapes in a different light. The result is a wine scene that is more diverse and exciting than ever before. In Tibilisi, old Soviet era [music] factories are being transformed into art hubs and techno clubs. DJs spin beats that echo the complex rhythms of our polyonic [music] folk music. Young designers are taking the geometric patterns of traditional textiles and weaving them into modern clothes. Even our food is changing. Chefs are deconstructing [music] kachapuri and kinkali, making them new again in restaurants that sit next to old family bakeries. It's like our culture is always in conversation with itself. >> Technology is also playing a crucial role. Georgian startups are developing apps to help tourists navigate the country's wine routes, while digital archives are preserving ancient manuscripts and musical recordings for future generations. Social media allows young artists and entrepreneurs to share their work with a global audience, showcasing a side of Georgia that goes beyond outdated stereotypes. This embrace of the new is not a rejection of the old. Rather, it is a confident assertion that Georgian culture is not a museum piece. It's a living, breathing, evolving entity as resilient and adaptable as the vines that grow in its soil. Georgia's story is a powerful testament to the endurance [music] of the human spirit. It is the story of a small nation that has weathered the storms of history, holding fast to its [music] language, its faith, and its unique cultural identity. From the ancient cave city of Uplitsiki to the modern art spaces [music] of Tibilisi, we see a continuous thread of resilience. This is [music] a culture that does not break. It bends, it absorbs, and it grows stronger. The mountains have given its people strength and the constant [music] flow of empires has given them a wisdom and adaptability that is truly remarkable. At the very heart of this story is the vine. The 8,000-year-old [music] tradition of wine- making is more than just agriculture. It is the lifeblood of the nation. The kvery buried in the earth is a perfect symbol of Georgia [music] itself. Rooted deeply in its own soil, drawing strength from its history and transforming [music] what it is given into something beautiful and lifeaffffirming. The survival of over 500 grape [music] varieties is a miracle of preservation, a living library of flavor and history [music] that is Georgia's unique gift to the world of wine. It reminds us of the value of diversity and the importance of protecting our shared natural heritage. The Supra with its eloquent tomada and its rituals of fellowship offers a profound lesson for our modern fast-paced world. It teaches us the importance of slowing down, of coming together, of communicating with honesty and heart. It shows how food and wine can be [music] powerful tools for building community and strengthening the bonds that tie us together. In an age of digital [music] disconnection, the Georgian Supra is a vibrant reminder of our fundamental need for face-to-face [music] human connection, for shared song, and for collective celebration. It is a feast for the soul that [music] nourishes our common humanity. Ultimately, George's message is [music] one of hope. It is a story of how a culture can remain true to its ancient roots while embracing the future with open [music] arms. It shows that tradition is not a static relic but a dynamic force [music] that can be reinterpreted and revitalized by each new generation. The enduring vine which first gave its fruit to [music] humanity in this very land continues to thrive. Georgia, the cradle of wine, offers a toast to survival, to identity, and to the indomitable spirit of life itself.

- Wine Law and Pricing Explained for WSET 3: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome to the explainer. Today we are unccorting two of the most important topics in wine. And if you're studying for something like the WCT level 3, this is absolutely for you. We're going to get to the bottom of why one bottle is 10 bucks and another is a thousand. And we'll decode all that legal jargon on the label so you know what it really means. All right, so let's just jump right in with the big question, right? You're standing in a wine shop looking at a wall of bottles. Why does one cost less than a sandwich and another costs more than a plane ticket? Well, I promise you it's not just fancy marketing. It's a really fascinating mix of costs from the vineyard all the way to the shelf and a whole world of regulations. So, here's how we're going to break it all down. First up, we'll look at the costs from the grape to the glass. Then, we'll follow the wine on its journey to the shelf. After that, we're going to get into the law on the label, specifically how it works in Europe. And finally, we'll put it all together and see exactly how price meets law. Okay, section one, from grape to glass. You see, the price of a bottle of wine starts building long before it's even wine. It really all begins out there in the vineyard with the land, the vines, and just how much work it takes to grow great grapes. You know, this right here shows you just how much the land itself can drive up the cost. If you've got flat vineyards, you can bring in machines to do almost everything, which keeps costs way down. But on some of those world famous steep slopes, like in the Mosul in Germany, no way. It's all done by hand. I mean, sometimes the vineyards are so steep that workers literally have to be tied on with ropes. That kind of manual labor is incredibly expensive. And you better believe that cost gets passed right into the bottle. And once those grapes finally get to the winery, the costs just keep piling up. I mean, first there's the huge expense of just setting up the vineyard and buying all the equipment. We're talking tanks, presses, the whole shebang. But then there's the cost of time itself, maturation. Think about it. If a wine maker is aging a wine in an oak barrel for 2 or 3 years, that wine is just sitting there taking up space and not making a single penny. That delay, that tied up cash has to be factored into the final price. All right, so the wine is made, it's been aged, we're done, right? Not even close. Getting that finished wine from the seller to your local shop adds a whole other set of costs to the equation. Yeah, this map of the supply chain really shows it all. Every single step here adds a little bit more to that final price tag. Take packaging. A super heavy, fancy, custom molded bottle costs a lot more to make and ship than a simple lightweight one. Then there's transport. Shipping wine across the globe costs a fortune compared to just driving it down the road. And of course, the importer, the distributor, the retailer, they all need to get their cut along the way. Oh, and let's not forget about the silent partner in every single bottle of wine you buy, the government. On top of all those other costs, you have taxes and duties. And we're not talking about pocket change here. In some places, taxes can actually be one of the biggest single parts of that final price you see on the shelf. It's huge. The kind of producer making the wine makes a big difference, too. You've got your estates who grow all their own grapes. They have total control, which is great for quality. But if a hail stom wipes out their crop, they're in big trouble. Then you have merchants who buy grapes or wine from others. This lets them blend for a consistent style year after year. And you can't forget cooperatives, which are super common in Europe. They're basically a collection of growers who pull their resources, which is a lifeline for small farmers. Okay, so we've got a handle on the costs. Now, let's switch gears and talk about the law. When you look at a wine label, what do all those words actually mean? What are they promising you? This is where we dive into the rules of the game. The single most important concept in wine law pretty much everywhere in the world is the geographical indication or GI for short. And really, it's simple. A GI is just a legally protected name for a place. It's a line drawn on a map that guarantees the grapes for that wine were grown inside that line. That's it. It's a promise of origin. Now, here's a number you absolutely have to know, especially for any wine exam. 85%. For a wine to legally put a GI on its label in most parts of the world, at least 85% of the grapes have to come from that specific named region. Remember that number. It's key. So, the European Union, which is, you know, the heartland of wine, takes this GI idea and kicks it up a notch. They've built a whole two-level system that creates a kind of quality pyramid. And you really need to understand how it works. And here are the two tiers. Think of it like this. PDO or protected designation of origin is the top of the pyramid. These are for smaller, more specific, often more famous areas and they come with a super strict rule book. What grapes you can use, how you can make the wine, everything. Then you have PGI, protected geographical indication. This is a much broader category. It covers larger areas and gives wine makers way more freedom and flexibility to experiment. But for that top tier, for a European PDO wine, the rule gets even stricter. It's not 85%, it's 100%. No wiggle room. Every single grape has to come from that defined region. It is an absolute guarantee of origin and authenticity. Now, here's a little tip. You're not always going to see the letters PDIO on the bottle, and that's because the EU lets countries use their own traditional terms. So in France you'll see Appalachian derision control A or AOC. In Italy you'll see the famous DOC or the even higher level DOCG. Just remember these are all different local names for that same top tier PDO category. Okay, last section. Let's bring these two worlds together. We've talked about money. We've talked about law. So how do these legal rules directly impact the price you pay for that bottle? Well, here's the absolute crux of it. When a wine region has a prestigious legal status, like a really strict PDO, those rules do a lot more than just protect its name. They actively restrict how much wine can be made. The laws might demand lower yields in the vineyard or dictate expensive production methods, which raises costs and crucially limits the total supply. And it all comes down to this. It's classic economics 101, really. You have a famous region that everyone wants a piece of. So demand is super high. But at the same time, the strict laws of that region keep the supply very, very limited. And what happens when high demand crashes into low supply? The price has nowhere to go but up. That right there is how law and price come together in the world of wine. So we'll leave you with this to think about. We've seen how the costs pile up and how the law creates scarcity. All of which builds that final price tag. But does that price born from all these rules and market forces actually guarantee that you're going to love what's in the bottle more than something else? Well, that's a question only you can answer. Thanks for joining this explainer.

Um and that creates uh kind of a viral way for wine makers to acquire new customers. Um, and then the consumer can then take that Vincoin that they receive for for opening the bottle uh in exchange for that data and they can spend it on more wine or experiences and build stats. So that's the first $10 billion problem. How do we solve customer acquisition, consumer data and loyalty? The second $10 billion problem is provenence. So, most people know that wine, especially luxury wine, needs to be stored and transported with the proper um temperature and humidity conditions or it'll spoil. Um you've probably all everyone that's listening, if you if you like wine, you order wine at a restaurant, um the wine comes and you'll you'll they'll they'll pour that little taste for you and you'll smell it. You'll be like, "Ah, this is this is off. It's not right." And that wine gets poured out. Well, as it turns out, that's about one in 10 bottles um ends up being a bad bottle. Ends up being spoiled, ends up being porked um and ends up being thrown away. And uh in a hundred billion dollar industry, that's a $10 billion problem. Um and that problem mostly ends up uh acrewing to the to the little restaurant owner, the little wine shop owner, um or even, you know, sometimes to the consumer. And that's a that's a big problem. Um and that's a problem that can be solved relatively easily. Now um for a long time people have been trying to solve that problem with a centralized solution. Meaning how can we create a way to to track bottles um track and track that the the temperature and humidity conditions as that bottle moves its way through the supply chain. But because the supply chain is so atomized, 10 million independent middlemen, um it's it was always impossible for any one player, for any centralized player to create a system that will track or monitor bottles on their way through the supply chain. However, blockchain and this is a this is a very new thing just in the last couple of years has enabled a new technology that we call deepen which is a decentralized physical infrastructure network. And what that means is instead of one company owning all of the the different owning the entire um system for tracking wine, it allows each individual node along the supply chain to be part of a big network um and to and to be rewarded for sharing just the information in their little island or in their little oasis. So we've partnered with and taken actually a strategic investment from the inventors of RFID. So RFID technology is it's that same technology where you know you go to the Amazon store and you put all your stuff in the in the shopping cart and you just walk out the door and as you're going through the door it analyzes everything in your cart and charges your credit card. Um that's RFID. Um, and it's a it's a wireless technology that's really good for tracking um individual items. Uh, a lot of times it's used in theft prevention. Um, but it's used in a in a lot of different ways. For a DPIN, the way that we're looking at it is each individual bottle of wine has an RFID tag. So, it's it's identified with a unique tag. And as that bottle moves through the supply chain, whether it's into or out of a warehouse, into or out of a temperature-cont controlled truck or a temperature controlled um shipping container or um into its final destination at a restaurant or or or a wine shop and then even on to a final consumer that each step along the way, there can be an independentlyowned RFID interrogator. That's what the reader is called. That can um uh that can update the blockchain entry, the the ledger entry for each individual bottle of wine with location, temperature, and humidity conditions. Now, um what that does is it creates a record for each bottle. Now, the question is how do you pay for it, right? How do you pay this giant network of of um RFID interrogators? Well, the way that we're looking at it is um that Vincoin attached to each bottle can make a micro payment every time that bottle goes through a warehouse or a temperature controlled shipping container or a temperature controlled truck or even into the final um destination, wine shop or restaurant. Um, and then even when it goes to the consumer, um, everywhere along that chain, the, um, the independent player can be paid, even if it's just 10 cents a bottle, to update the location and conditions of that bottle. So at the end of the road, the the retailer or the wine shop or even the consumer can scan that bottle and see the entire chain of custody all the way from the wine maker to the consumer or what we say from from grape to glass. Um, and you know, by by looking at that information, by seeing the the temperature and um uh and and humidity conditions in the location, the they're able to get like the the big green thumbs up or maybe the red thumbs down, and they get to know whether or not that bottle was was was taken care of or was was properly um stored and transported. And that will what what that will do is create incentives all the way through the supply chain to have proper provenence. Um and we think that not only saves the industry $10 billion a year, but more importantly it saves a lot of great wine from being poured down the drain. Um and and I'm all for that. Um so how that works really simple. You know between um the the winery and the consumer that wine usually has many hops. um in each hop a little bit of money, a little microtransaction um in Vincoin pays for the for the um the location and um temperature humid humidity uh uh conditions to be updated on that bottle. So by the time you get to the end, you know that bottle's authentic. You can see the chain of custody and you can see the provenence of that bottle. Now, there's a third $10 billion problem um uh in the wine industry, and that's um and that to me is actually one of the most interesting problems and also a problem um that that really the blockchain was sort of invented to solve. So when you think about international transactions, um what you typically think about is one Fortune 500 company buying $5 million worth of raw materials from another Fortune 500 company, right? It's a big transaction. It's, you know, a full container full of, you know, sugar or parts or whatever. And those transactions are safe and cheap and efficient. and they've become safe and cheap and efficient over the course of the last 30 years, it's very simple to to send um products uh overseas. But when you think about the wine industry and because it's so atomized, instead of the typical international transaction being, you know, a big company buying $5 million worth of wine, it's actually a little tiny winery in France sending five cases of wine to a little tiny importer in Norway. And instead of a $5 million transaction, that's a $2,000 transaction. And that transaction instead of being instead of over the last 20 years becoming more and more efficient, it's actually becoming significantly less efficient. So that little $2,000 transaction has a bunch of problems. The big, you know, number one problem is counterparty risk, right? instead of two big players with a big amount of money where there's recourse if you send the wrong thing or you make a mistake or someone tries someone doesn't send the money, you know, you can sue them because for $5 million it makes sense. But when you have two little players, like how do I know you're going to send me the money? How do I know you're going to send me the wine? And if you don't, what am I going to do about it? Am I really going to hire a lawyer to sue you for $2,000? Probably not. So, there's real counterparty risk in in those transactions. There's also huge fees. The wine maker um uh or I'm sorry, the the importer when they're sending the money back, they're probably paying, you know, the same uh international wire fee as that $5 million transaction. But on a $2,000 transaction, it's taking a pretty big bite out of every bottle. On the other side with the wine maker, you know, they're they probably because they're a small company, they haven't uh don't have the ability to negotiate better rates with their bank. So, not only are they suffering through long international wire settlement delays, but they're also paying the worst possible FX rate. So, um, when you add all of those things up, we did a a little study with Deote last year on a $50 bottle of wine, about $6.10 is just going to risk and banks, which means that when you open a great bottle of wine, the first glass that you pour goes to your banker. And we don't think that makes very much sense. Uh, we think that there's many, many more efficient ways. And when you think about the blockchain and what it was created to solve, you know, this counterparty risks, that's a smart contract. You know, if you've got a smart contract that that defines um the transaction and automatically makes payment, that really gets rid of counterparty risk. if you have digital currencies um that settle in two seconds instead of five days and provide the very best um FX rate versus the worst um that solves a you know if it's if it's $610 if it's 12% of every bottle of wine and it's a hundred billion dollar industry that's another $10 billion problem that we can solve with by just using simple blockchain and digital transactions. So, um, we're we're that's that's kind of the the last problem that we're looking to solve. We and and just to to make sure that everybody knows, we we don't think that any of these problems get solved by, you know, stodgy old wine makers or stodgy old wine consumers, you know, getting a a a MetaMask wallet and buying a bunch of crypto. Like, we don't think that's what happens. Um, and in fact, if you if you look at um at the consumer app that we've developed, devin.app, it looks a lot more like your Delta Sky Miles app than it does like any kind of crypto app. Um, and on the wine maker side, um, you know, we're not really looking to bring the wine makers into web 3. We're looking at plugging web 3 into the existing wine maker systems. So, um, you know, plugging into their plugging using an API to plug into their existing accounting systems, their existing ERP systems, their loyalty systems, uh, or their loyalty platforms or even their direct to consumer sales platforms. Um, just plugging web 3 into it. So, they're doing things the same way that they've been doing it for years, but more efficiently, less expensively, more profitably, um, by using web 3 components. um you know when they're when the the wine maker is creating the going into the accounting system to create the invoice for that little that little importer in Norway instead of you know creating a paper invoice that they email they're building a smart contract um and the little importer in Norway instead of connecting their bank using Plaid they're using an on-ramp system that we've developed with Deote and Amazon managed blockchain and Fireblocks um and they don't even know that they're that they're using, you know, digital currencies. But what they do know is that everything's cheaper, everything's faster, everything's more efficient, and everything has less risk. So those are the three big $10 billion problems, loyalty and um and and customer acquisition and consumer data. Um the second is provenence that we're solving with DPIN. And the third is DeFi uh is making transa international transactions easier and faster. Um and the the the added benefit of that is by turning wine from a uh possession into an asset now you also have the ability to access all kinds of other DeFi um uh um DeFi protocols or DeFi um uh benefits. For example, one of the things that I get talked about uh that people ask me about the most is creating credit facilities. So now I know when they think about it, they think, man, I I'd love to be able to go to a wine or to go to a wine collector that has a half a million dollar collection and let them borrow against that wine collection. And if it's tokenized and um and we have transparent pricing and transparent liquidity um it's very easy to create a credit facility for you know a wine collector to borrow against their half a million or million dollar wine collection. But where it starts to get really interesting is wine makers themselves. Right? So in Bo in uh in in Piamonte in Italy um in a very very small region though by local regulation the wine makers need to age their wine for four years in barrel and then four years in bottle before they're allowed to sell it. That's eight years worth of inventory that every wine maker has in their seller. Well, instead of being an asset because they're not allowed to sell it and they're required to store it, that 8 years worth of inventory is a liability. But if it was tokenized now they can borrow against it for um for for operating uh uh operating cash requirements. They can sell futures. They can hedge against it. I I can only imagine the all of the DeFi products that will be created for wine makers to use um to create to make their businesses more efficient as soon as their main asset is tokenized. Um so we're really excited about that. Um, I'm going to talk. So, this is how the uh the the those international transactions work. - How Can I Understand My Personal Wine Palate?: Kind: captions Language: en Have you ever tasted a wine that just clicked with you, leaving you wondering why it felt so right? Or perhaps one that just did not quite resonate with your taste buds? Finding those perfect sips is a wonderful journey. Understanding your personal wine palette means actively exploring, paying close attention to your sensory experiences, and then identifying the specific characteristics that you consistently find enjoyable. It is about discovering what makes a wine truly sing for you. The why behind your palette is deeply personal, rooted in a fascinating blend of biology and experience. Your unique set of taste receptors, your past dietary preferences, and even your emotional state can influence how you perceive different wines. Factors like a wine sweetness, its acidity, which creates that refreshing zing, the textural sensation of tannins often found in red wines, and the overall body or weight of the wine in your mouth. All combined to form its unique profile. Learning to recognize these elements is your first step to unlocking your preferences. So, how do you put this into practice and truly pinpoint what you love? Start by consciously tasting a wide variety of wines. Do not just stick to one type. When you taste, do not just swallow. Take a moment to really think about what you are experiencing. Is it fruity, earthy, spicy, or floral? Is it light-bodied or full-bodied? Does it feel smooth or tart on your tongue? Take simple notes, even if it is just a mental check-in, about what you liked and what you did not. A fantastic method is comparative tasting, where you try to different wines side by side to highlight their differences in a very clear way. As you explore, you will begin to notice patterns. Maybe you always gravitate towards wines with higher acidity or those with soft, mellow tannins. Understanding these preferences helps you articulate your tastes more clearly, perhaps to a sier or when browsing at a wine shop. Think of it like building your personal flavor profile. You might find you prefer the bright fruit and zesty finish of a Sovenon blank or the rich complex layers of a Cabernet Sovenon. Your palette is not static either. It evolves just like your life experiences. So continued exploration keeps your wine journey fresh and exciting. Ultimately, investing this time into understanding your personal wine pallet offers a profound reward. It empowers you to make confident choices that perfectly match your desires, leading to greater enjoyment with every glass. It transforms wine from a mystery into a source of consistent pleasure, ensuring each bottle you open is a delightful experience tailored just for you.

- What Is the Best Way to Provide Wine Tasting Guidance? - Fine Wine Facts: Kind: captions Language: en What is the best way to provide wine tasting guidance? Imagine walking into a room filled with different wines and feeling unsure how to really understand what you're tasting. That's where good guidance makes all the difference. To help someone truly appreciate wine, you need a clear and simple approach that teaches them how to observe, smell, taste, and describe what they experience. Starting with a structured method like the wine and spirit education trust systematic approach to tasting or the court of master sier's deductive tasting method is a great idea. These frameworks guide tasters step by step through examining the wine's appearance, aroma, taste, and overall quality. For example, the W set method encourages using a shared vocabulary to describe flavors and textures, making communication easier. The court of master sier's method often involves blind tasting, which sharpens sensory skills and helps tasters focus on what they are experiencing without bias. Teaching tasters to evaluate wines based on the forky qualities of balance, length, intensity, and complexity is very helpful. Balance looks at how well the wine's acidity, tannins, alcohol, and sweetness work together. Length is about how long the flavors stay in your mouth after swallowing. Intensity refers to how strong the aromomas and flavors are, and complexity means how many different layers of taste and smell you can find. Encourage people to pay close attention to the wine smell first, noting any aromomas and checking for faults. On the pallet, they should feel the texture. whether it's smooth, silky, or rough. And notice the wine's body, which depends on alcohol and glycerin levels. Explain how barrel aging adds flavors like vanilla or toast from oak, and how grape varieties influence expected aromomas and tastes. Using comparative tastings or blind tastings helps tasters recognize what makes a wine typical of its region or grape and what makes it stand out. Developing sensory memory is key. So, encourage recalling familiar scents and flavors to improve recognition. Practical tips include using clean glasses, serving wines at the right temperature around 55° F for most wines, and pouring small amounts to avoid waste, reminded tasters to take breaks, spit out samples to prevent fatigue, and cleanse their pallet with neutral foods like bread or water. In the end, guiding someone through wine tasting involves combining a repeatable process with sensory education and practical tips. This helps tasters become more confident and enjoy wine more fully, whether they are beginners or seasoned enthusiasts. [Music]

- Get a behind-the-scenes look at our blind tasting process. 🍷: Kind: captions Language: en have you ever wondered what rating wine really looks like everybody in the wine business wants their wine professionally tasted and rated and that's where we come in once the wine arrives we process the wine into our system and ensure that the details are recorded into our database it's not as easy as just popping the bottle and tasting it we let the wine properly rest in our Cellars this is to ensure that the wine is tasted at its optimal profile before we taste the wine the wines are checked for Flaws our wines are tasted with our standard tasting glass then the bottles are placed in paper bags to ensure there is no bias each reviewer of our tasting team specializes in a specific region and they are also calibrated to raid according to the parameters of our 100 point scale system finally the wine is tasted blind no we're not blindfolded but the wine bottle stays hidden until after the rating is given we do not drink it we taste it we taste well over 100 Wines a day to check our latest scores click the link in our bio cheers

  18. @old_blues2397: Okay I see the comments are so good; perhaps I am the strange one here but I feel differently. Here's my honest opinion of the whole thing:

The scenery is amazing. What a shot and well chosen location to tell such a story but....but the fact that something is visually appealing and pleasingly doesn't necessarily make its content great. 
The fact that a cake looks beautiful on the outside doesn't mean the inside is well baked. 

Let us analyse: 

So the movie is about 3 sisters trying hard to not only save their grandfather from debts but also salvage the legacy he has created over the years that appears to be his life. He is for authenticity and guards it jealously with his heart. To be honest, this is a fantastic plot and I love it but... what actually happened? It was not just well executed!

So they decide to rent out parts of their home to guests to live in for vacation. The best the movie makers could do was come up with a twist that 3 guys rent the place and fall in love with all 3 sisters.... I am not so sure of that arrangement. It sounds like a lazy tension heightening attempt to me. It could have been planned differently. But... but if the writers wanted to keep such a premise then they should have carefully executed it in a much better way but they did not. The best they did was to find 3 weak immature boys to fill up the spots. No!!! Did anyone not realise the 3 sisters where a bit too matured to be handled by such boys? I am not saying old I am saying matured and I was struggling to see the connection and love. No synergy; everything out of place!
It looked like "hey this is a love movie and if 3 boys find 3 ladies you know naturally there would love" no! This is a horrible outcome and very uncreative and lazy. How can you waste and pair up such a picturesque location with a weak lazy driven plot like this? So so flat, no curves.

Again, I find that the cast were struggling with dialoguing. It's as if the writers did not know what to write for the cast to say. The dialogues were painfully mundane and flat. I am not talking about accents, I mean the substance of the conversations. 

Again people will fight me because the scenery is perfect so it somewhat covers up the imperfections I am addressing but if this movie was acted in a different setting you would have hated it. 

The whole thing felt like being served bland food but somehow you're forced to eat it with a smile because it is at a Michelin Five Star restaurant so you pretend you like it just to look and sound elegant.

I would not want, per say, the story idea to change. I think it has great potentials and perhaps in the right hands it can be a tantalising love movie story you wouldn't wish to end but....

First of all let's get more matured actors to fill the spot of these 3 boys so as to match these women. 

Secondly, let us figure out a much more interesting way to deliver this romance story of 3 men hiring a lodging to experience an Italian countryside vacation and let us burn the love more intensely and give it some depth.

Thirdly, I think a strict grandfather with a gun can be better written. Not a bad twist to the entire story but it can be given a better facelift to make it more interesting than annoyingly flat. 

And if you want wine to be the heart of it, aside love, the writers could have been more proactive with the scripting; featuring much more meaningful elements to build up an unforgettable winery moment. I don't think I got to appreciate the quality wine element of the grandpa at all. You cannot do that to such a plot. Let us feel the specially guarded treasure wine legacy - but no.

I hate that with all these potentials available to the production they chose mediocre knowing well this tastes bland; no spice. And I hated the whole thing - absolutely. 

If not for the cinematography, scenery and the fact that the story is not so bad I would have called it trash!

There’s a place out there so elite that you basically need to be a celebrity or incredibly wealthy just to get a personalized tasting with the owner. And that kind of exclusivity is exactly why everyday folks feel alienated by these places.
On the flip side, we have people like André Mack, a down-to-earth sommelier who became known through the Bon Appétit YouTube channel. He brings wine culture to the average person and makes it accessible and relatable. It’s people like him who open doors rather than close them, and that’s exactly the kind of attitude that earns respect from the broader community. 

Some might argue that keeping things exclusive for the upper class is intentional—I get it. There’s a certain allure in catering to a niche crowd. But in reality, a smaller vineyard selling moderately priced wines consistently over time is often far more profitable. Those ultra-rare, one-bottle sales might make headlines, but the vineyards offering quality bottles at $10 to $50 a pop are playing the long game and bringing in way more steady revenue. It’s the difference between a one-off luxury splash and sustainable, inclusive success.

- What Are The Best Rare Wine Tastings At Napa Valley? - We Go To Festivals: Kind: captions Language: en What are the best rare wine tastings at Napa Valley? Imagine walking into a room filled with the most exclusive wines that only a few get to taste. Napa Valley is known for its world-class wines, but some are so rare they are almost impossible to find. During the festivals and special events, you get a chance to sample these hidden gems. One of the top experiences is the Howell Mountain Harvest Celebration. This event brings together over 40 wineries from the Howell Mountain American Viticultural Area. Many of these wineries are not open to the public normally and produce limited hard to get wines. You can taste and even buy these wines directly from the producers. The event takes place at the Culinary Institute of America at Copia and includes gourmet food and a silent auction with exclusive winery experiences. It is a favorite for those wanting to discover rare wines from this mountain region. Another mustsee is the auction Napa Valley. This is a big yearly event where wineries auction off rare wines and special experiences. Attendees can bid on large bottles known as magnums or even larger formats and enjoy dinners hosted by famous venters. The auction raises money for local charities and offers a chance to taste some of Napa's most sought-after wines. Then there is Festival Napa Valley's Taste of Napa. This event features over 90 wineries, many offering limited edition and rare vintages. Guests can sample these wines alongside culinary delights, making it a perfect way to find new favorites. Some wineries host intimate harvest parties and dinners where you can try rare vintages poured from large bottles. For example, Balachi Vineyards hosts a harvest cabernet dinner with exclusive pores from select vineyard blocks. These events often include conversations with the wine makers, giving you insight into how these special wines are made. Private estate and wine maker series tastings are also popular. Wineries like Axern Napa Valley offer small educational tastings focusing on single vineyard wines. These are often not available elsewhere and give a deep look into teroir and craftsmanship. Invitationonly events like Premier Napa Valley are the most exclusive. They gather the top wineries to showcase their rarest and most collectible wines. These tastings are highly limited and often requiring special invitations. Overall, these rare wine tastings are a big part of Napa Valley's festival scene. They combine the J of tasting exceptional wines with the excitement of discovering something truly unique. Whether it's through a large festival or an intimate private event, these experiences give visitors a chance to enjoy wines that are usually reserved for collectors and connoisseurs. It's a chance to celebrate Napa's rich wine- makingaking heritage while tasting some of the most exclusive bottles in the world.

means more oxygen or more age one more thing about site that i think is important and as a big misconception are the legs you'll see a lot of people looking at this and then looking at the tears coming down the streaks and thinking that has something to do with quality but actually doesn't have anything to do with quality at all it really only has to do with alcohol content but since we're thousands and thousands of smells it's the same reason why when you have a cold you can't taste anything your nose is all blocked up so this to me is the most important step first thing we're going to do is we're going to swirl now i know this looks a little pretentious but the reason we swirl has some science behind it alcohol evaporates quicker than water and so when a little bit of that alcohol evaporates it carries those aromas up into your nose so you can do this test at home smell a wine that's just been sitting for a while and then swirl it and smell it and i guarantee you're going to get different results so that's your first step your second step is to get your nose just at the point where you can smell you don't want to get too far in there if you get too far in there you'll end up kind of burning your nose with the alcohol smell i think if you ever smelled like a really hard like vodka or something and we don't want to do that we want to get just enough to get the the aromas it taste the same as it smelled or are you getting new flavors are you confirming what you smelled are you getting different things and the second and most important is what is the structure of the wine there are five elements to the structure of a wine we can go in depth on these in fact i do in other videos but for now let's just talk about the basics number one is acid how much acidity does the wine have acid is found in things like lemons and vinegars it's kind of that brightness that pop maybe a bit of a sour note let's talk about does it have low acid or medium acid or high acid the next step we're going to talk about is the alcohol so when you taste it and it goes down your throat does it burn do you taste the alcohol is it a high alcohol lime or is it a bit lower next is the body body is how rich and how weighty the wine feels in your mouth a lighter bodied wine think of a skim milk a medium bodied wine think of two percent and think of a full bodied wine as whole milk how rich does it feel in your mouth the next part is tannin now tannin only occurs in red wines it comes from the skin stems and seeds of the grapes and it's that element in a red wine that dries your mouth out so a lot of people mistakenly call this dry but it's not dry that's tan instruction it'll dry out the roof of your mouth and your gums it gives the wine a firmness or a backbone does it have a lot of that character or does it have little or none and the last part is sugar now sugar is kind of misinterpreted a lot of times most the wines we taste are dry they don't actually have any sugar in them they might be fruity but they're not sweet a sweet wine a lot of rieslings can be off dry or sweet a lot of dessert wines like pork can be sweet but for the most part most of the wines we taste in our everyday life are dry they don't actually have any sugar in them and those five elements are what makes up your palate and we've made it to step four the final step of wine tasting which is the analysis it's three parts number one is this wine balanced so does the acid balance with the alcohol bounds with the sugar is a structurally sound wine or is something out of place does it drink hot or is it too tart for you number two is the length of the wine how long after you drink it does it stay and you continue tasting it usually a better wine the longer after you take a sip you'll continue to get new flavors and continue to taste it and number three how complex is it when you're coming up with your flavors when you were smelling and you were tasting did you get a whole bunch or did you get uh it tastes like apples and citrus and that's about it or did you get new things every time you took a sip did it keep going and changing is it opened up in the glass so that's the complexity so you take all those elements together and then you judge our quality of the wine whether we like it or don't like it and that's it in four easy steps you just tasted i know that was a lot and this is a long video but the reality is once you get good at this and once you practice this a lot of this becomes second nature and you don't actually have to do this with every single wine i mean i have tons of wines in my everyday life you know they're cheap 15 wines 10 wines and i don't need to analyze them like this but there's also wines where i do want to spend a little more time with maybe i spent a lot of money maybe it's a special wine to me and i want to get the most out of it and so i hope this gave you a road map of how to do that i hope you guys found this video useful and if you did do me a favor give me a thumbs up and put any questions you have in the comments so i can answer and follow along with your tasting journey i'm doing another video where i talk about the master sony exam and what it takes to become a sommelier so definitely check that out thanks for watching and until next wine you

- 10 WINE MISTAKES & How to AVOID THEM like a Master.: Kind: captions Language: en hi i'm constantinbaum master of wine and from the outside one of them seems complicated or even intimidating so i want to share with you how to avoid the following 10 mistakes in order to enjoy one like a master well that sounded a bit corny but you know what i mean so let's go wine mistake number one drinking white wines too cold and red wines too warm i've been talking about this issue for ages but no one seems to be listening white ones are usually stored in the fridge while red wines are stored somewhere in an apartment white wines are therefore often poured at six degrees celsius which is way too cold and red wines are often put at 20 25 30 degrees celsius depending on the weather which is way too warm what i would recommend is take your white wine out of the fridge like 30 minutes before you want to serve it well you should actually put your red wine into the fridge half an hour to an hour before you want to pop the cork i actually often drink my whites and my rats at the same temperature because they're all stored down here in the cellar so they're at around 16 degrees celsius when i take them out i would recommend serving most red wines at 17 degrees celsius where you should serve most white wines at 10 degrees celsius really good wines should be served not cold or hot but at a cool temperature because that's how they can express themselves really cold wines don't smell like much and really warm wines often just smell like alcohol wine mistake number two pouring too much wine into your glass i always wanted to try this but you can see you can almost fit an entire bottle into one of those bigger glasses but the problem with that is that you don't leave enough space on top of the wine in order to really allow it to show its full aromatic potential this still smells good though so you should always make sure that there's enough wine in the glass so that you have something to drink but not too much of it so this is pretty much the maximum of wine that you should have in your glass more would be excessive and you can always pour in more once you drink some here's a bonus mistake that you shouldn't make when learning about wine not subscribing to my channel please do subscribe mistake number three is believing that you need expensive equipment in order to get you started in wine you don't really need a lot of expensive stuff i might have a lot of pricey toys today but when i first started practicing for the master of wine i often tasted from ikea glasses and i still sometimes use those 3b corkscrews that you get from wine producers or wine producing regions of course you might get a little bit more flavor from a fancy glass or you look cooler when you use one of those expensive corkscrews but it's better to invest that money into wine in order to broaden your palette you can also get creative in order to save money who needs an expensive decanter when an old jam jar basically does the same job and this comes at a much much lower price so just try it mistake number four judging a wine by the first sip tasting wine is like getting to know a new person sometimes your new best friend needs a little bit of time to get out of his shell and it's the same with some more delicate wines they just need time to fully express themselves on the other hand they're also ones that seem really impressive on the first sip but get boring after one or two classes so take your time and don't dismiss or celebrate a wine before the bottle is empty mistake number five not judging a wine by its cover yes you heard me right it's a mistake not to judge a wine by its cover of course when drinking wine it's all about the content of the bottle but when you're buying wine and you don't get to taste it it's also really important that you check out the label the label tells you a lot about the origin the grape variety the alcohol content and sometimes even on the rating of a wine but it also gives you lots of information on the intent of the producer this producer for example looks very classic classical they don't care about modernizing they already have their customer base locked in so they don't have to make the label easier to understand while this producer on the other hand has spent quite a lot of time and probably also money on designing that label in a way that it's easy to understand that it works on a shelf and also so that consumers remember the name the brand of that wine mistake number six taking food pairings too seriously when matching wine and food there are only a few mistakes that you can really make you should not eat something really sweet with a less sweet or dry wine and you shouldn't eat something really spicy with a very alcoholic wine but that's about it of course if you want you can go really deep into food and wine matching but for me most of the times it's really just important that i drink really good food and really good wine did i just say drink really good food well you know what i mean on top of that that's how you find new great matches do you think anybody ever sat down at a desk for hours thinking about possible combinations for blue cheese and then came up with port no somebody said at a table had a bottle of port open and had some blue cheese there and then realized wow that really works mistake number seven to just drink what you like i know this is a bit controversial because lots of wine people now tell you to just follow your palate and of course you should find out what you like but in order to do that you should listen to friends somebody's wine experts pretentious wine snobs and of course taste around you cannot really know what you like without having tasted many wines before and most wine experts get to taste way more wines than you therefore the independent advice of a knowledgeable taster is valuable in order to get you to find better wines faster of course you shouldn't religiously follow anybody's advice and believe that a wine is good just because taster x gave it a big score mistake number eight being intimidated by the big labels you should have respect for wine and especially wines with a long history should get some respect from you however just because the wine was great for centuries doesn't mean that it's still good i remember many instances where i tasted a famous wine and was disappointed especially when considering the price of that product on top of that maybe you just don't like this style somebody   8. @Chris-hf2sl: Wine temperature - it's not as simple as he makes out. After 30 years of wine tasting, I've come to the conclusion that more acidic white wines such as Riesling and Sauvignon Blanc, along with sparkling wines, need to be colder than less acidic ones. When it comes to reds, the heavier it is, the warmer it can be, though never higher than 20ºC. In contrast, lighter reds such as Pinot Noir need to be a few degrees colder.

Yeah Rudy K was busted but there are plenty of them.

I have imported wine into SE Asia before, though these days, I'm largely retired - and wish I'd kept some of the DRC wines I've consumed over the years (along with a few others, e.g., Rousseau, Leroy, Jayer, Coche Dury).

Maureen is absolutely correct.  
A wine importer I'm friends with gets offers of fake wines pretty well all day long.
One offer was a supply of DRC that amounted to more than the entire production of the domaine.
The cheaters clearly didn't check first.
Yet in many parts of Asia, and manly China, fake everything is a big business from watches, bags, shoes, athletic wear, etc, down to eggs, meat, mineral water and of course wine.
If you think it's only the top wines like first growth Bordeaux and GC Burgundy, you will get a shock.
Now I've owned shares in the parent companies (now TWE) of Penfolds since the 80's and know them pretty well.
So when I see labels with the wrong colour, spelling errors and ink that rubs off, you don't need to be too smart to work out that even the lower end wines are fake.
In Shanghai some years ago, I saw obviously fake Penfolds wines at a supposedly well to do retailer.
When I complained, they angrily denied it. Not surprised really.

- How Do You Find Aged Nebbiolo Wine Beyond Specialty Stores? - Fine Wine Facts: Kind: captions Language: en How do you find aged Nebola wine beyond specialty stores? Imagine tasting a wine that has spent years developing its character, revealing layers of flavor you might never find in a young bottle. Finding aged nebio outside of specialty stores can seem tricky, but it's definitely doable with a few smart strategies. Start by exploring online wine retailers and auction sites. These platforms often carry a wide selection of older vintages from the Piedmont region, especially from well-known producers like Barolo and Barbaresco. Look for detailed descriptions, ratings, and provenence information to ensure the wine has been stored properly. Some online shops specialize in rare and vintage wines, making it easier to find bottles from the 1970s or 1980s. Next, consider participating in wine auctions. These can be live or online, and they often feature collectible bottles from prestigious vineyards. Bidding on these wines requires some research, but it can be a great way to access limited editions or older vintages that are not available in retail stores. Keep an eye on auction houses that deal specifically with fine wines and check their cataloges regularly. Another option is to buy directly from wineries or join wine clubs in the Piedmont region. Many wineries keep a stock of older vintages in their sellers or offer library wines to members. Joining a wine club can give you access to exclusive releases and special bottlings that are not sold elsewhere. Contact wineries directly to inquire about purchasing aged Nebio bottles or attending tastings of their older vintages. High-end restaurants and wine bars are also good sources. Some establishments sell bottles from their private collections or have a selection of aged nebiolo by the glass or bottle. Building a relationship with a sumier or wine director can open doors to these rare finds. They often know where to source older vintages or can recommend upcoming tastings of aged nebio wines. Finally, engaging with private collectors and wine enthusiast communities can lead to private sales or seller visits. Attending wine events or joining local wine clubs can connect you with people who have carefully stored nebulo bottles over the years. These private sales often involve wellpreserved bottles that have been kept in optimal conditions. Remember, age nebiolo is prized for its complex aromomas like leather, truffle, dried fruit, and spice. Proper storage is key to maintaining quality. So, always verify provenence and storage history before buying. When you find a well-kept bottle, you get to experience the grape's evolution from a bold, tanic young wine into a refined, elegant masterpiece. In short, beyond specialty stores, you can find aged nebio through online shops, auctions, direct winery purchases, upscale restaurants, and private collector sales. Each of these options opens a door to discovering the rich layered flavors that only time can develop in this remarkable red wine varietal. [Music]

- What Is A Negociant In Rare Wine? - Luxury Life Report: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] What is a negotiant in rare wine? Have you ever wondered how some of the rarest wines make their way to collectors and connoisseurs? One key player in this world is the negotiant. So, what exactly is a negotiant in rare wine? A negotiant is a specialized wine merchant who purchases grapes, juice, or unfinished wine from various growers, often small family-owned vineyards. They then blend, bottle, market, and sell the wine under their own label. Unlike vineyard owners who manage both grape growing and wine- making, negotiants focus on the wine- making, blending, and distribution stages. This system is particularly common in French wine regions like Bordeaux and Burgundy where vineyard holdings can be small and fragmented. In the context of rare wine sourcing, negotiants play an important role by accessing rare and highquality wines from multiple small producers. Many of these producers might not have the resources to bottle or market their wine internationally. This means that high- netw worth individuals can acquire exceptional wines that might otherwise be hard to find. Negotiants also offer a broader selection and consistency. By blending wines from different vineyards or vintages, they help maintain quality and supply stability even when harvest conditions vary. This is especially beneficial for collectors who want to ensure they have a reliable source of fine wines. Additionally, negotiants provide a gateway to prestigious appellations without requiring direct contact with often private or exclusive chatau owners. In Bordeaux, for example, the negotiate system has historically acted as the primary channel for distributing classified growth wines. This means that collectors can access some of the finest wines without needing to navigate the complexities of vineyard relationships. Furthermore, negotiants enable investment and funding for vineyards by purchasing wine or grapes in advance, a practice known as n primur. This supports vineyard owners financially and helps sustain the production of luxury wines. From a luxury lifestyle management perspective, negotiants are valuable partners in sourcing rare wines. They can offer exclusive access to limited production bottles, often at prices more competitive than buying directly from the vineyard. Their knowledge in blending and aging means the wines they provide can be of exceptional quality and uniqueness. This appeals to collectors who are looking to add distinguished bottles to their sellers. In essence, a negotiant is a key figure in the rare wine market. They bridge the gap between small-scale growers and luxury wine consumers, facilitating access to rare, collectible wines while ensuring quality and availability through their merchant and wine-making activities.
